did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning approach for skin disease classification. Specifically, it employs a dual-stage EfficientNet-B3 model with transfer learning, focal loss, and morphological segmentation to classify dermoscopic images from the HAM10000 dataset into seven classes including melanoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a hybrid deep learning approach for skin disease classification. It details a specific methodology (dual-stage EfficientNet-B3, morphological segmentation, partial oversampling), describes the dataset (HAM10000), and reports quantitative performance metrics (82.3% accuracy, 65.0% balanced accuracy) and ablation studies. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICDSINC), it contains original technical contributions and empirical validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Hybrid Deep Learning Approach for Skin Disease Classification Using Adaptive Segmentation and Dual-Stage EfficientNet
journal: 2025 1st International Conference on Data Science and Intelligent Network Computing (ICDSINC)
brief_summary: This study addresses skin disease classification from dermoscopic images by proposing a hybrid deep learning pipeline that combines morphological segmentation, partial oversampling, and a dual-stage EfficientNet-B3 model. The approach targets challenges such as severe class imbalance and confounding artifacts like hair and scale bars, utilizing the HAM10000 dataset comprising 10,015 images across seven classes. Evaluated using a patient-level data splitting strategy, the method achieved 82.3% accuracy, 65.0% balanced accuracy, and a 66.9% macro F1-score. Ablation studies demonstrated that the two-phase training strategy contributed a 3.6% accuracy improvement, while the complete pipeline enhanced balanced accuracy by 13.5 percentage points over the baseline. Notably, the melanoma F1-score improved from 0.509 to 0.586, indicating significant gains in detecting this critical malignancy despite persistent confusion with benign nevi.

lesion_types_studied.details: Minority class in HAM10000 dataset; 48 images in test set; 4.8% prevalence.
Minority class in HAM10000 dataset; 72 images in test set; 7.2% prevalence.
Minority class in HAM10000 dataset; 156 images in test set; 15.6% prevalence.
Minority class in HAM10000 dataset; 26 images in test set; 2.6% prevalence.
Malignant class in HAM10000 dataset; 168 images in test set; 16.8% prevalence. F1-score improved from 0.509 to 0.586.
Majority benign class in HAM10000 dataset; 671 images in test set; 51% prevalence. Primary source of confusion for melanoma (mel→nv).
Minority class in HAM10000 dataset; 22 images in test set; 2.2% prevalence.

all_task_types.target: Seven discrete skin disease classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc) from dermoscopic images.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model outputs a probability vector p̂ = [p̂1, ..., p̂7] for the seven classes, and the final prediction is determined by ĉ = arg max p̂k. The task is explicitly defined as 'Skin lesion classification' and 'automated dermoscopic screening'.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, used as the primary dataset for this study.

use.training: Used for training the dual-stage EfficientNet-B3 model after morphological segmentation and partial oversampling of minority classes.
use.validation: Used for validation (approximately 1,000 images) to monitor performance during training.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation (1,002 images) to report accuracy, balanced accuracy, and F1-scores.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To prevent data leakage by ensuring no patient appears in multiple partitions (train, validation, test).
To systematically evaluate the contribution of individual components (segmentation, oversampling, two-phase training, focal loss) to the overall performance.
To analyze per-class performance, including precision, recall, and F1-score, and to identify primary confusion patterns (e.g., melanoma to nevus).
To evaluate the trade-off between sensitivity and false positive rates, particularly for minority classes like melanoma.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance improvements were described using absolute percentage point differences (e.g., +3.6% accuracy, +13.5 percentage points balanced accuracy) and relative improvements (e.g., 15.1% enhancement in melanoma F1-score) derived from ablation studies and comparisons against a baseline, but no statistical significance testing was performed.

HAM10000.evaluation_setting: Full Pipeline (Morphological Segmentation + Partial Oversampling + Two-Phase EfficientNet-B3 with Focal Loss)
HAM10000.notes: The full pipeline improved balanced accuracy by 13.5 percentage points over the baseline. Melanoma F1-score improved from 0.509 to 0.586 (15.1% enhancement). Two-phase training contributed +3.6% accuracy individually. Oversampling boosted balanced accuracy by +5.2 percentage points but decreased overall accuracy by 2.4 percentage points. Segmentation showed minimal individual impact (-0.4 percentage points) but enhanced performance when combined. Primary confusion pattern was melanoma misclassified as nevus (mel→nv). Melanoma recall was 61% (102/168) with 50% precision. df had the best minority class performance with 88% recall.

architecture_details.scaling: Compound-scaled architecture scaling network depth, width, and resolution
architecture_details.backbone_freezing: Phase 1: Backbone frozen; Phase 2: All layers unfrozen
architecture_details.classifier_head: 7-class classification head (outputting probability vector p̂ = [p̂1, ..., p̂7])

training_details.oversampling: Partial oversampling of minority classes to 50% of the gap to majority class size (nnew = nc + 0.5 × (nmax − nc))

preprocessing_techniques: Grayscale conversion of input color images

Adaptive threshold-based segmentation using Otsu's method for low-contrast images (adaptive threshold τ_adaptive) and fixed low-intensity thresholding (typically τ=20, or τ=119 for specific cases) to create binary masks
Morphological closure using a 3x3 structuring element to fill gaps caused by hair artifacts
Component selection via 8-connectivity to identify the largest connected component (lesion)
Bounding box extraction and cropping of the lesion region from the original image
Resizing cropped images to 300x300 pixels for EfficientNet input
Confidence-based fallback: defaulting to full-frame inference when segmentation confidence C_seg < 0.7
Partial oversampling of minority classes to address class imbalance (n_new = n_c + 0.5 * (n_max - n_c))
Data augmentation during training: spatial augmentations (random flips p=0.5, rotations θ ∈ [-30, 30], shifts ±5%) and color augmentations (brightness α = 1 ± 0.2, contrast β = 1 ± 0.2)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random flips with probability p = 0.5, rotations within range θ ∈ [-30, 30] degrees, and shifts of ±5%.
Brightness adjustment with factor α = 1 ± 0.2 and contrast adjustment with factor β = 1 ± 0.2.

Minority classes were oversampled to 50% of the gap to the majority class size, calculated as n_new = n_c + 0.5 × (n_max - n_c), where n_c is the class size and n_max is the size of the majority class (nv).

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning pipeline, combining morphological segmentation, partial oversampling, and a dual-stage EfficientNet-B3 model, effectively addresses class imbalance and confounding artifacts in skin disease classification.
The full pipeline achieved 82.3% accuracy, 65.0% balanced accuracy, and a 66.9% macro F1-score on the HAM10000 dataset using patient-level data splitting.

The two-phase training strategy (freezing backbone then fine-tuning) contributed a 3.6% accuracy improvement individually, while the complete pipeline improved balanced accuracy by 13.5 percentage points over the baseline.

Melanoma detection performance significantly improved, with the F1-score increasing from 0.509 to 0.586 (a 15.1% enhancement), demonstrating the method's capability to detect critical malignancies despite class imbalance.

Partial oversampling of minority classes boosted balanced accuracy by 5.2 percentage points but resulted in a 2.4 percentage point decrease in overall accuracy, highlighting the trade-off in handling imbalanced data.

Morphological segmentation alone had minimal individual impact on performance (-0.4 percentage points) but enhanced results when combined with other techniques, helping to isolate lesions from hair and background artifacts.
The primary source of error was the confusion between melanoma and benign nevi (mel→nv), with melanoma achieving 61% recall but only 50% precision, indicating significant false positives.
The approach offers a practical and reproducible solution for automated dermoscopic screening, particularly for minority classes, though challenges remain in distinguishing melanoma from nevi.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have significant source bias (images from different clinics/devices), limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging protocols.
No external validation was performed; the authors explicitly note that evaluation on independent datasets like ISIC 2019 or PH2 is needed to assess real-world deployment robustness.

Melanoma detection performance remains suboptimal with a precision of only 0.55 and recall of 0.52 (F1-score 0.586), indicating a high false-positive rate where melanomas are frequently confused with benign nevi (mel→nv).

The dataset exhibits severe class imbalance, with the majority class (Nevus) comprising 51% of the test set, while rare classes like Vascular lesions (2.2%) and Dermatoﬁbroma (2.6%) have very few samples (22 and 26 respectively), potentially skewing model reliability for these classes.

The morphological segmentation approach is heuristic-based (thresholding and closure) rather than learned, which may fail on images with complex artifacts or low contrast, triggering a fallback to full-frame inference in 6% of cases.

Partial oversampling was used to address class imbalance; while it improved balanced accuracy by 5.2 percentage points, it caused a 2.4 percentage point decrease in overall accuracy, highlighting a trade-off between minority class recall and majority class precision.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported; performance improvements are described using absolute percentage point differences from ablation studies.

The model was evaluated using patient-level splitting to prevent data leakage, which is a strength, but the small test set size for minority classes (e.g., 26 for df, 22 for vasc) limits the statistical power of per-class metrics.

The paper does not report computational efficiency metrics (e.g., inference time, FLOPs) or model size, which are critical for assessing deployment feasibility on edge devices or mobile dermoscopes as suggested in future work.
